dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a reported Cardiac Arrest/Death on --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Marcy, an EMS EMT with Rescue Squad 3. What happened here today?
I came out of the bathroom and found my husband unresponsive and not breathing. He's on the floor near the bed.
Does he respond to loud noises or physical stimuli at all?
His eyes aren't opening. They're closed and still.
Any coughing, grunting, or speaking when we talk to him?
No, he hasn't said anything or made any noises.
Any movement in his arms or legs when you gently touch them?
No, he's completely still.
His airway is patent with no visible obstruction.
I don't see anything in his mouth or airway that would block breathing.
He's not breathing on his own. Let me confirm with a breath sound check.
No spontaneous breathing observed. Do you want to assist with ventilation?
Yes, moving to assisted breathing using a BVM.
No spontaneous respirations recorded at this time.
Placing a nasopharyngeal airway to secure the airway now.
Confirmed. NPA in place. Airway remains open.
Attaching four-lead ECG for continuous monitoring. Let me place the leads.
ECG on standby.
Initial rhythm appears absent.
I'll check for pulse. No pulse felt at the radial or carotid artery.
Pulse is not detectable.
Blood pressure is unobtainable at the moment.
His skin is diaphoretic and shows signs of cyanosis.
SpO2 is unobtainable at this time using non-invasive methods.
Continuing BVM assisted breaths at high oxygen flow.
Mask and BVM secured with assisted ventilations.
SpO2 now at 57% with BVM on 15L O2. Improvement noted.
Starting IV access on the left wrist now for medication preparation.
IV successfully placed with 18-gauge catheter and secured with a sterile technique.
Can you describe what he was doing just before he became unresponsive?
I was in the shower. When I came out, he was on the bed, purple, and not moving.
When was the last time you saw him awake or alert?
Maybe about 30 to 45 minutes ago.
Has he had a history of drug use or abuse?
Yes, he's struggled with heroin and cocaine in the past.
Based on presentation and suspicion of opioid toxicity, giving first dose of naloxone now.
First naloxone bolus of 0.25 mg administered via IV.
Capnography now at 70-80 mmHg. Demonstrating signs of return of spontaneous respirations.
Pulse now recorded at 95, and remains in sinus rhythm.
Observing 12 assisted breaths per minute. Gradual signs of breathing.
Blood pressure showing 123/61 now.
EKG now in sinus rhythm.
SpO2 at 67 percent. Continuing O2 management.
Glucose measurement shows elevated at 282 through a venous stick.
Noting improvement in skin color and moisture. Skin is now warm to touch.
Peripheral pulses are bounding and equal.
Pain is not assessed at the moment due to unresponsiveness.
Administering second naloxone bolus, 0.25 mg via IV.
Oxygen saturation now at 97 with continued improvement.
Breathing rate now 24 per minute with clear return.
Glucose levels remain elevated. No drop seen in second assessment.
Pupils are now reactive to light and appear equal in size.
Skin condition is now normal, showing no signs of trauma or rash.
Initiated Protocol 7.1 for opioid overdose, patient is recovering consistently.
Patient has improved to A&Ox4 with GCS of 15 now.
Adjusting O2 to nasal cannula at 3 LPM due to current clinical status.
BP at 163/105, pulse 95, and still in normal rhythm.
Current SpO2 at 99%. No sign of respiratory distress.
The patient is responding to verbal orders with some confusion and can follow simple commands.
Would you consider being transported to a hospital for further evaluation?
No. I feel better. I'd rather stay home and continue to monitor myself.
Understood. I will respect your decision but continue to monitor you through treatment.
Are you open to accepting some information about recovery support services?
Yes, I would like that information.
I'll leave you with this recovery packet containing helpful contacts and information. Also, I'm securing the IV as a saline lock now.
Final vitals: SpO2 99%, BP 163/105, pulse 86.
Opioid overdose likely; patient is now awake and oriented to time and place, A&Ox4 with GCS 15.
Two naloxone doses administered. IV secured as a saline lock, NPA, and ECG in place.
Denied transport and chose to remain at home. Advised to monitor for symptom recurrence.
Calls to 911 for further assistance advised if symptoms worsen or recur.
IV secured, NPA left, ECG still attached but no transport performed.
Recovery packet provided. EMS interventions now complete.
All protocols and procedures completed safely with aseptic techniques and two naloxone doses given.
Patient remains afebrile and stable at time of handoff.
Patient declined further hospital evaluation and is not showing further distress at this time.
Emergency team observed full return of vital signs and orienting response post-naloxone.
Breathing rate now at 18 as well. Stable and continued improvement.
Oxygen saturation still at 99%, with no observed respiratory compromise.
Final pulse ox 99, and no significant acid-base drift noted in capnography.
Patient's respiratory rate now stable at 18 per minute.
All findings support stabilization with positive airway and oxygenation management.
Naloxone administered appropriately with clear reversal of opioid effects confirmed.
IV access remains in situ for potential re-dosing if needed.
Significant improvement observed with no worsening condition during field contact.
All EKG leads remain applied and showed sinus rhythm during monitoring.
No trauma or signs of advanced cardiac deterioration noted during secondary exam.
Patient remains alert to surroundings and is taking measured actions on introductions.
All clinical findings and responses consistent with opioid overdose reversal.
Patient understood benefits of transport and opted to remain at home.
Clinical contact complete. EMS will complete on site as per patient discretion.
All protocols concluded and documented within the medical record.
Patient reports no pain currently.
